text: The surgeon begins the hernia repair by taking down attachments to the semilunar line to access the preperitoneal space, using a 30-degree camera for visualization. Medial dissection is performed down to Cooper's ligament and the pubic bone.
The surgeon carefully navigates the anatomical layers to reach the peritoneum, aiming to identify the gonadal vessels early in the procedure for a large indirect sliding hernia repair.

The surgeon carefully dissects to avoid unnecessary bleeding, enhancing visualization of the tissue plane. The fourth robotic arm assists in the dissection as the surgeon approaches the peritoneum, identifying and sweeping the gonadal vessels and cord structures off the sliding retroperitoneal component of the large indirect hernia. The sigmoid colon is visible within the hernia sac.

The surgeon attempts to reduce the entire hernia sac, carefully navigating through fibrotic tissue layers. The hernia sac appears complex with additional layers, and the surgeon cautiously avoids damaging the bowel protruding through the defect.

The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia sac, successfully freeing a large portion from the fibrotic capsule. The cord structures are visibly swept posteriorly and laterally, ensuring their safety. The surgeon identifies the VAS to safely take down the remaining hernia sac, carefully navigating through a scarred segment in the medial aspect of the canal.
The surgeon checks the surgical field and continues with the parietalization of the cord structures, completing the dissection at the left end of the inguinal region.

The camera is positioned to the left of midline, above the umbilicus, providing a view into the canal where the cord and vas are visible without any cord lipoma. The surgeon finishes creating the pocket and prepares to place a 16 by 12 pro grip mesh inside, noting the ample space available.
The surgeon places an NG catheter to evacuate gas from the flap, revealing a smooth and well-positioned surgical area.
